skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Turn Remotion component code into rendered MP4 video files.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: The static external command and network findings are mostly true positives for intended functionality: the skill enables Bash access to the inference.sh CLI and sends rendering jobs to a remote service. No prompt injection, credential theft, or malicious persistence was found. The weak cryptographic algorithm matches are false positives from descriptive text and table content, so the skill is publishable with a clear network and code-submission warning.

medium_findings.title: Remote CLI Rendering Sends User Code to External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for infsh commands and demonstrates rendering TSX code through infsh/remotion-render. This is legitimate for the skill, but user code and props are sent to a remote service, so sensitive code or private data could leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and examples directly show infsh command execution with TSX code input. The behavior is core functionality, not malicious, but the privacy and remote execution implications are clear.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Asset URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh, cloud.inference.sh, and Remotion documentation. They support documentation and display content, with no evidence of credential exfiltration or hidden endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or image links, and no secret values are appended to them. Network use still matters because the skill depends on a hosted rendering service.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to crypto code. The cited areas contain description text and an input schema table, including the word codec.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, or authentication logic at the cited lines. The static match appears caused by prose rather than executable cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can invoke the infsh CLI through Bash. Commands are scoped to infsh, but users should understand that rendering requires local CLI execution and remote service calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command permission and command examples are explicit. The scope is limited to infsh, which lowers severity but confirms the pattern.

content.user_title: Render Remotion Videos from React Code
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to turn React animation code into video files. This skill uses the inference.sh CLI to render Remotion TSX components into MP4 output.
actual_capabilities: Runs infsh commands for the infsh/remotion-render app.
Accepts React and Remotion TSX code that exports a default component.
Supports width, height, FPS, duration, codec, composition ID, and props inputs.
Demonstrates common Remotion APIs such as AbsoluteFill, Sequence, spring, and interpolate.
Shows Python SDK usage for normal and streaming render progress workflows.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Sends submitted component code and props to the inference.sh rendering service.
Does not include a local Remotion renderer or local video build pipeline.
Only documents imports from React and Remotion, so other package support depends on the remote app.

use_cases.title: Generate branded motion clips
use_cases.description: Create short MP4 clips from reusable React components with controlled size, duration, and frame rate.

use_cases.title: Render data-driven videos
use_cases.description: Pass props into a Remotion component to produce videos that reflect changing campaign, product, or reporting data.

use_cases.title: Automate video export experiments
use_cases.description: Use CLI or Python SDK examples to test several animation variants without opening a video editor.

prompt_templates.title: Render a simple title card
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner render with basic Remotion code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use remotion-render to create a 3 second MP4 title card. Use a 1920 by 1080 canvas, 30 FPS, and a fade-in headline.

prompt_templates.title: Create a square social animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 1080 by 1080 Remotion video for a social post. Animate a counter from 0 to 100 over 5 seconds at 60 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Render a prop-driven video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use remotion-render with props for title and subtitle. Render a 3 second product announcement video in MP4 format.

prompt_templates.title: Stream a longer render
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Python SDK streaming pattern for remotion-render. Render a 10 second animation and report progress until the video URL is available.

output_examples.input: A prompt asks for a 3 second fade-in title card at 1920 by 1080.
output_examples.output: The skill runs the inference.sh Remotion render app and returns a completed MP4 video location.

output_examples.input: A prompt asks for a square animated counter with 60 FPS output.
output_examples.output: The skill submits TSX component code with render settings and produces a square MP4 suitable for social media.

output_examples.input: A prompt asks to render a video using title and subtitle props.
output_examples.output: The skill sends the component and props to the render service and returns the generated video result.

best_practices: Review TSX code before rendering because it is sent to a remote service.
Start with short durations and lower resolution while testing animation timing.
Keep props explicit so repeated renders are easy to audit and reproduce.
anti_patterns: Do not submit secrets, private source code, or confidential customer data as props.
Do not assume local rendering because the workflow uses inference.sh services.
Do not render long videos before checking component behavior with a short test.

faq.question: What does this skill render?
faq.answer: It renders React and Remotion TSX components into MP4 videos through the inference.sh CLI.

faq.question: Does it require Remotion knowledge?
faq.answer: Basic Remotion knowledge helps because the input must export a valid default component.

faq.question: Can I control resolution and FPS?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented inputs include width, height, FPS, duration, codec, composition ID, and props.

faq.question: Does it run completely locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the infsh CLI and the inference.sh remote rendering app.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential code?
faq.answer: Use caution. The component code and props are submitted to an external rendering service.

faq.question: Can it show render progress?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a Python SDK streaming example for progress updates.
